description: Expansive park & former royal hunting ground, with small hills, streams & a boating lake.
address: P.º de la Prta del Ángel, 1, 28011 Madrid, Spain
short_address: P.º de la Prta del Ángel, 1, Madrid
emails_found: contenidos@esmadrid.com, correo@ejemplo.com, dpd@madrid-destino.com, publicidad@esmadrid.com, registro@madrid-destino.com, turismo@esmadrid.com
review_1_text:
Casa de Campo (translating to country house in Spanish) is a large public park located to the west of the historic centre of Madri. The park is over 1,700 hectares in size and is the largest park in Madrid.

The park was originally a royal hunting estate and was created by King Philip II in the 16th century - from which the park gets its name. The estate was originally used primarily by Spanish royalty for leisure and was only opened to the public in 1931. This was due to the transition of Spain from a monarchy to a new Republican government which aimed to modernize Spain and break from class privilege and divide.

Today the park features some spacious and beautiful forests and gardens and rolling hills, it is a great spot to take a break from busy city life and go for a walk. Due to the quantity of hills there are a lot of points from which visitors can appreciate panoramic views to the rest of the park and even back over to the city skyline. There are a lot of cycling options available too including a covered cycleway. There are other curiosities to find in the park too such as remaining Spanish Civil War bunkers and the Valdeza Aqueduct.

Lago (lake) is a great spot for a picnic or for eating at one of the several lakeside cafes and is a nice entry point to visit the park. Metro access is available by the Lago station on line 10 of the Madrid Metro. Casa de Campo also boasts the Madrid Parque de Atracciones and the Zoo Aquarium de Madrid.
review_2_text: It's not just a park, a forest. Get a bike from the bicimad (€0.5 for 30 mins), then ride a bike inside of the park. There are many water fountains in the park, but I didn't see any restroom. Maybe you can use the restaurants' wc around the lake. That's why I give 4-stars.
review_3_text:
must visit place for cycling/running/hiking in madrid.a perfect park for everybody. and I mean everybody. you'll deffo find something you enjoy there.

for bicycling, you can rent a biciMad from right across the street near the metro station pricipe pio

review_1_text: I came to this toy store because I thought they had some really fun things, but all that goes out the window when you get home and are served by a very rude woman. She doesn't care if you're a customer or an employee; she yells at the other staff in front of you, and even worse, she treated an elderly man who's worked there for a long time terribly. I went in to exchange a toy that didn't work, and she treated me like I was stupid, saying maybe I put the batteries in wrong, that the brand of batteries should be different, and that the toy worked and they'd give me a refund anyway. The delivery was supposed to be yesterday, Monday, and today, Tuesday, I haven't received anything. My child is left without his toy, and I don't think that's right. It's really awful.
review_2_text:
This is the second time I've been to this toy store, and I've encountered a very rude, curly-haired saleswoman, who is disrespectful not only to her colleagues, whom she clearly treats with contempt, but also to the customers.
When I went through the checkout with a remote-controlled car, her response was, "That's all you're getting," with a disgusted look on her face. Well, yes, ma'am, it's my purchase, and you have no right to comment on it.
Looking through the other reviews, we noticed there was a whole section dedicated to this woman, where all the opinions were negative. I don't understand why she's still working with the public.
review_3_text: I'm giving one star. Today I went to buy toys for my two children and nieces, but the girl on duty this morning told me I couldn't bring in my six-month-old baby's stroller. She said it would make things fall over and that she'd have to pick it up herself. She also rudely told me I had too much stuff. I explained that my son had a soccer game, so I was carrying his backpack, a small shopping bag, and my baby's diaper bag. She said she had to keep an eye on everything. I didn't mind her keeping an eye on it, but I didn't like the way she spoke to me, so I told my older son we were leaving. Then the girl said, "That's better," which isn't the right way to talk to a customer.

review_1_text: I was lucky enough to spend three months in Madrid and attend regular classes with Gabi. It was an incredible experience, and Monday mornings were one of my favorite times of the week! Classes fly by because you enjoy them so much. Gabi's explanations and advice are a pleasure. In the workshop, you have absolutely everything you need, and the atmosphere is super calm (like a two-hour meditation, but making cute things!). I was able to finish five pieces (I'll add the photos), and in them, I was able to experiment with various modeling techniques, clay mixing, different types of glazes, and so on. And Gabi always let me unleash my not-so-ordinary ideas. The result is that I was incredibly sad to have to leave Madrid and skip regular classes. In short: anyone who wants to take ceramics classes or a weekend workshop, don't think twice and go to Neko Ceramics Workshop because it's a 10/10! Thanks again for everything, teacher!
review_2_text: I've been attending regular classes every Tuesday for a couple of months now, and it's become my favorite day of the week. Gabi is a highly experienced teacher, as well as incredibly kind and patient! The workshop is an ideal, intimate space with all kinds of tools.
